_schema: Business banking platforms for trades/contractors — Phase 2 category added 2026-05-28. These are fintechs (FDIC-insured via partner banks), not chartered banks. Grouped by banking_focus. APYs/fees verified-as-of verified_date and change often; re-verify quarterly. IMPORTANT trades caveat: most of these do not accept cash deposits, which matters for cash-handling trades. affiliate_url stays 'TBD' until tracking confirmed.

providers.name: Found
providers.tagline: Banking built for the self-employed — taxes and bookkeeping included
providers.pricing_summary: Core banking is free; Found Plus is $35/mo ($315/yr) and Found Pro is $80/mo ($720/yr) for advanced bookkeeping and tax tools. A fintech (banking via Lead Bank, Member FDIC), not a chartered bank
providers.apy: No high-APY angle — built around automatic tax set-aside, not interest
providers.long_description:
Found is business banking designed specifically for the self-employed — the solo contractor, handyman, or one-person trade business juggling 1099 income, quarterly taxes, and bookkeeping. NerdWallet named it the best business checking for paying contractors and saving for taxes, and that's exactly its lane: it's a fintech (banking provided by Lead Bank, Member FDIC) that bundles a checking account with the tax and bookkeeping tools a solo operator actually struggles with.

The standout feature is automatic tax set-aside: Found estimates your quarterly taxes and automatically moves money aside every time you get paid, so you're not scrambling at deadline. It also does expense tracking and categorization, contractor payments with no per-contractor fees, invoicing, and tax-form collection — all in one app, so a solo operator doesn't need separate banking + bookkeeping + tax tools.

The core product is free, with optional Found Plus ($35/mo or $315/yr) and Found Pro ($80/mo or $720/yr) adding advanced bookkeeping and tax features. The trade-offs: Found is built for solo/self-employed, so it's not the fit for a growing multi-employee shop that needs payroll, multiple users, or business credit — and like most fintech-banking platforms, it isn't a chartered bank and (typically) doesn't handle cash deposits, which matters for cash-taking trades. But for a 1099 solo trade who wants banking that handles the tax headache automatically, Found is purpose-built.
providers.how_it_works: You open a free Found account (a fintech account, FDIC-insured through Lead Bank). As money comes in, Found automatically estimates your self-employment taxes and sets aside the right amount into a tax bucket, so the funds are there at quarterly deadlines. It tracks and categorizes expenses for write-offs, lets you send unlimited contractor payments with no per-contractor fee and collect their tax forms, and handles invoicing — all in one app. The core is free; paid tiers (Plus/Pro) add deeper bookkeeping and tax tools. Because it's self-employed-focused, it's optimized for one-person finances rather than multi-user business operations.

pros_detail.title: Automatic tax set-aside
pros_detail.explanation: Found estimates your quarterly self-employment taxes and automatically sets money aside as you get paid — directly solving the #1 financial pain point for 1099 trades and removing deadline scrambles.

pros_detail.title: Banking + bookkeeping + tax in one app
pros_detail.explanation: Instead of separate banking, bookkeeping, and tax tools, a solo operator gets expense tracking, write-off categorization, invoicing, and tax forms alongside the checking account — a genuine simplification.

pros_detail.title: Free core product
pros_detail.explanation: The core banking and tax features are free, which is ideal for a solo contractor watching costs. Paid tiers are optional, only adding advanced bookkeeping/tax depth.

pros_detail.title: Contractor payments with no per-payee fee
pros_detail.explanation: Send unlimited payments to subs/contractors with no per-contractor fee and collect their tax forms, which is handy for a solo who occasionally pays helpers without a full payroll system.

pros_detail.title: Purpose-built for 1099 income
pros_detail.explanation: NerdWallet's pick for paying contractors and saving for taxes — Found is optimized end-to-end for self-employment income rather than retrofitted from a general business account.

cons_detail.title: Built for solo, not growing teams
cons_detail.explanation: Found is designed for self-employed/1099 operators. A growing shop with employees, payroll, multiple users, or business-credit needs will outgrow it and need a fuller business bank.

cons_detail.title: Not a chartered bank
cons_detail.explanation: Found is a fintech with banking via Lead Bank (Member FDIC). Deposits are FDIC-insured through the partner bank, but it's not a traditional bank relationship — understand how that works before moving all your cash there.

cons_detail.title: Cash deposits are a gap
cons_detail.explanation: Like most fintech-banking apps, Found isn't built for cash deposits — a real limitation for trades that still take cash. If you collect cash regularly, confirm your options before relying on it.

cons_detail.title: Advanced features cost extra
cons_detail.explanation: The deepest bookkeeping/tax tools sit on Found Plus ($35/mo) and Pro ($80/mo). The free tier is solid, but power users pay for the full feature set.

cons_detail.title: Limited credit/lending
cons_detail.explanation: Found focuses on banking + taxes, not working-capital credit. Contractors who want a line of credit alongside banking should look at Bluevine instead.

providers.best_for: Solo and 1099 trades operators (handymen, solo cleaners, single-operator contractors) who want banking that automatically handles quarterly taxes and bookkeeping in one app
providers.weaknesses: Built for solo/self-employed, not growing teams; not a chartered bank (banking via partner); cash deposits are a gap; advanced bookkeeping/tax features require paid tiers

ratings.reddit_sentiment: well-liked by 1099/solo operators; NerdWallet's pick for paying contractors and saving for taxes

providers.affiliate_payout_note: No confirmed affiliate program — we currently earn nothing from Found

faqs.q: Who is Found best for?
faqs.a: Solo and self-employed trades operators — a one-person handyman, cleaner, or single-operator contractor — who deal with 1099 income and want banking that automatically estimates and sets aside quarterly taxes, plus bookkeeping and contractor payments in one app. It's not built for growing multi-employee shops.

faqs.q: How much does Found cost?
faqs.a: The core banking, tax set-aside, and bookkeeping features are free. Optional paid tiers — Found Plus at $35/mo ($315/yr) and Found Pro at $80/mo ($720/yr) — add advanced bookkeeping and tax tools. Many solo operators run on the free tier.

faqs.q: Is Found a real bank?
faqs.a: Found is a financial technology company, not a chartered bank — banking services are provided by Lead Bank, Member FDIC, so deposits are FDIC-insured through the partner bank. That's standard for fintech-banking platforms, but it's a different relationship than a traditional bank, so understand it before moving all your operating cash there.

faqs.q: Can I deposit cash with Found?
faqs.a: Cash deposits are a known gap for most fintech-banking apps, Found included. If your trade still takes meaningful cash, confirm your deposit options before relying on Found as your only account — this is a common limitation across Found, Novo, Relay, and similar platforms.

faqs.q: Found vs a traditional business bank account?
faqs.a: Found wins for solo/1099 operators because of automatic tax set-aside and built-in bookkeeping — things a traditional bank doesn't do. A traditional bank (or a fuller platform like Bluevine or Relay) wins once you have employees, need business credit, take a lot of cash, or want multi-user access. Match the tool to where your business is.

providers.name: Novo
providers.pricing_summary: Free business checking — no monthly fee, no minimum balance, no transaction or incoming-wire fees, with out-of-network ATM-fee refunds (up to ~$7/mo). Only notable fee is a $27 NSF charge. Built-in invoicing, Reserves, and software integrations. A fintech (banking via partner bank)
providers.apy: No standard APY; offers Reserves for setting funds aside
providers.long_description:
Novo is a free, digital-first business checking account aimed at small businesses and entrepreneurs, serving 175,000+ small-business owners. Founded around 2015 by Michael Rangel and Tyler McIntyre and headquartered in Miami, it's a fintech (banking via a partner bank) that strips out the fees traditional banks charge: no monthly fee, no minimum balance, no transaction fees, no incoming-wire fees, and it even refunds out-of-network ATM fees. The only notable charge is a low $27 insufficient-funds fee.

For a small or mobile trades operator, Novo's appeal is simple: free checking with a clean app, built-in invoicing, 'Reserves' for setting money aside (taxes, materials), and solid integrations with business software. It's a no-frills, low-cost place to keep operating cash and get paid.

The trade-offs are depth and two specific gaps. Novo's customer support draws criticism (limited and slow), and — importantly for trades — it doesn't accept cash deposits, which is a real problem if your business takes cash. It also has no meaningful APY and limited credit/lending. For a small, card-and-digital trades business that wants free, integration-friendly checking and doesn't handle much cash, Novo is a strong value; cash-heavy shops or those wanting high APY, lending, or hands-on support should look elsewhere (Bluevine for APY/credit, a traditional bank for cash).
providers.how_it_works: You open a free Novo business checking account online (a fintech account via a partner bank). There's no monthly fee, minimum balance, or transaction fee, and out-of-network ATM fees are refunded up to about $7/month. You get a debit card, built-in invoicing to bill and get paid, 'Reserves' to bucket money aside (e.g., for taxes or materials), and integrations with common business software (accounting, payments). You manage everything from the app. The main constraints: no cash deposits, and the only notable fee is a $27 NSF charge.

pros_detail.title: Genuinely free, low-fee checking
pros_detail.explanation: No monthly fee, no minimum balance, no transaction or incoming-wire fees, plus ATM-fee refunds. For a cost-conscious small trades operator, Novo removes nearly all the fees a traditional bank charges.

pros_detail.title: Built-in invoicing
pros_detail.explanation: Invoicing is built into the account, so a small operator can bill customers and get paid without a separate tool — convenient for a one-or-two-person shop.

pros_detail.title: Software integrations
pros_detail.explanation: Novo integrates with common business software (accounting, payments), so it fits into a small business's existing stack more easily than a basic bank account.

pros_detail.title: Reserves for setting money aside
pros_detail.explanation: The Reserves feature lets you bucket funds (taxes, materials, profit) within the account — a lightweight cash-management aid for operators who don't need Relay's full multi-account system.

pros_detail.title: Low NSF fee
pros_detail.explanation: The only notable fee is a $27 insufficient-funds charge — low compared with traditional banks — so the cost of an occasional slip is modest.

cons_detail.title: No cash deposits
cons_detail.explanation: Novo doesn't accept cash deposits — a serious limitation for trades that still take cash payments. If cash is part of your business, Novo can't be your only account.

cons_detail.title: Customer support is weak
cons_detail.explanation: A recurring complaint is limited, slow customer support. For your operating bank account, support quality matters when something goes wrong — set expectations accordingly.

cons_detail.title: No meaningful APY
cons_detail.explanation: Novo focuses on free checking, not interest. If earning yield on idle balances matters, Bluevine (up to 3% APY) or Relay's tiered savings are better.

cons_detail.title: Limited credit/lending
cons_detail.explanation: Novo is checking-first with limited credit/lending. Contractors who want a working-capital line alongside banking should consider Bluevine instead.

cons_detail.title: Fintech, not a chartered bank
cons_detail.explanation: Like its peers, Novo is a fintech with banking via a partner bank. Deposits are FDIC-insured through that bank, but understand the structure before centralizing all your cash.

providers.best_for: Small, card-and-digital trades businesses that want free, integration-friendly checking with built-in invoicing and don't handle much cash
providers.weaknesses: No cash deposits (a real problem for cash-handling trades); weak customer support; no meaningful APY; limited credit/lending; fintech (banking via partner bank)

ratings.reddit_sentiment: valued as free, integration-friendly checking; common complaints about support and the lack of cash deposits

providers.affiliate_payout_note: No confirmed affiliate program — we currently earn nothing from Novo

faqs.q: How much does Novo cost?
faqs.a: Novo business checking is free — no monthly fee, no minimum balance, no transaction fees, no incoming-wire fees, and it refunds out-of-network ATM fees (up to about $7/month). The only notable fee is a $27 insufficient-funds charge. It makes money on other services rather than monthly fees.

faqs.q: Can I deposit cash with Novo?
faqs.a: No — Novo doesn't accept cash deposits, which is a real limitation for trades that take cash. If cash payments are a meaningful part of your business, Novo can't serve as your only account; you'd need a traditional bank (or a workaround) for cash handling.

faqs.q: Is Novo good for contractors?
faqs.a: It's good for small, card-and-digital trades businesses that want free checking, built-in invoicing, and software integrations, and that don't handle much cash. Cash-heavy shops, those wanting APY or a credit line, or those who need strong customer support should consider Bluevine, Relay, or a traditional bank instead.

faqs.q: Does Novo pay interest?
faqs.a: Novo doesn't offer a meaningful APY — it's a free-checking play, not a high-yield account. It does have a Reserves feature for setting money aside. If earning interest on your balances matters, Bluevine (up to 3% APY) or Relay's tiered savings are better fits.

faqs.q: Is Novo a real bank?
faqs.a: Novo is a financial technology company, not a chartered bank — banking services are provided through a partner bank, and deposits are FDIC-insured via that bank. That's standard for the fintech-banking category (Found, Relay, Bluevine work similarly), but understand the structure before centralizing all your operating cash.

providers.name: Relay
providers.tagline: Multi-account business banking built for Profit First cash management
providers.pricing_summary: Starter free (0.91% savings APY); Grow $30/mo (1.55% APY); Scale $90/mo (2.68% APY). Up to 20 checking accounts + 2 savings under one login, 50 debit cards, FDIC up to $3M via Thread Bank's deposit-sweep program. No minimum balance
providers.long_description:
Relay is business banking built for cash management — specifically the Profit First method, where you allocate every deposit across dedicated accounts for taxes, owner pay, materials, and profit. Founded in 2018 (Bain Capital Ventures-backed) and providing banking via Thread Bank (Member FDIC), it lets you open up to 20 checking accounts and 2 savings accounts under one login, each with its own account/routing number, plus up to 50 debit cards with individual limits. It topped at least one 2026 ranking of best business bank accounts.

For a trades business that struggles with cash flow — not knowing how much of the bank balance is really 'theirs' versus owed for taxes and materials — Relay's multi-account structure is genuinely useful: split each customer payment automatically so the tax money is physically separated before you spend it. Pricing scales from a free Starter plan (0.91% savings APY) through Grow ($30/mo, 1.55%) and Scale ($90/mo, 2.68%), with no minimum balance, and FDIC coverage up to $3M via Thread's deposit-sweep program.

The trade-offs: Thread Bank received an FDIC enforcement action in 2024, and some users report occasional sudden account freezes with slow resolution (funds remain FDIC-insured) — a risk worth noting, though common across fintech banking. And like its peers, cash deposits are limited. For a contractor who wants disciplined, multi-account cash management and no-fee banking, Relay is the standout; cash-heavy operators and those wanting a single simple account may prefer alternatives.
providers.how_it_works: You open a Relay account (banking via Thread Bank, Member FDIC) and create up to 20 checking accounts and 2 savings accounts under one login, each with its own account/routing number. You then allocate incoming deposits across them — the Profit First approach of immediately moving set percentages into tax, owner-pay, materials, and profit accounts so the money is separated before you spend it. You can issue up to 50 debit cards with individual limits/restrictions for staff. Savings tiers earn 0.91%/1.55%/2.68% APY depending on plan (Starter free / Grow $30 / Scale $90), with FDIC coverage up to $3M via Thread's deposit-sweep. There's no minimum balance.

pros_detail.title: Multi-account Profit First structure
pros_detail.explanation: Up to 20 checking accounts under one login let you split every deposit into tax, owner-pay, materials, and profit buckets automatically — solving the cash-flow clarity problem that sinks many trades businesses.

pros_detail.title: No-fee Starter plan
pros_detail.explanation: The Starter plan is free with no minimum balance and 0.91% savings APY, so a contractor can adopt disciplined multi-account banking without a monthly cost.

pros_detail.title: Tiered savings APY
pros_detail.explanation: Savings APY scales to 1.55% (Grow) and 2.68% (Scale), so as you keep reserves (tax/materials buckets), they earn meaningful interest rather than sitting idle.

pros_detail.title: Up to 50 debit cards with controls
pros_detail.explanation: Issue cards to staff with individual spending limits and merchant restrictions — useful for a crew-based trades business controlling field spending.

pros_detail.title: High FDIC coverage
pros_detail.explanation: FDIC insurance up to $3M via Thread's deposit-sweep program (vs the standard $250K) protects larger balances — reassuring for a contractor holding tax and materials reserves.

cons_detail.title: Thread Bank enforcement + freeze reports
cons_detail.explanation: Relay's partner, Thread Bank, received an FDIC enforcement action in 2024, and some users report sudden account freezes with slow resolution. Funds remain FDIC-insured, but it's a real operational risk — keep a backup account.

cons_detail.title: Limited cash deposits
cons_detail.explanation: Like other fintech-banking platforms, Relay isn't built for cash deposits. Cash-handling trades will find this a meaningful limitation for an operating account.

cons_detail.title: Best value needs a paid plan
cons_detail.explanation: The higher savings APYs (1.55%, 2.68%) require Grow ($30/mo) or Scale ($90/mo). The free Starter is fine for the multi-account structure, but top yield costs a monthly fee.

cons_detail.title: Multi-account setup takes discipline
cons_detail.explanation: The power of Relay comes from actually using the multi-account/Profit First system. If you won't set up and maintain the allocations, a simpler single account (Novo) may serve you better.

cons_detail.title: Fintech, not a chartered bank
cons_detail.explanation: Relay is a fintech with banking via Thread Bank. Deposits are FDIC-insured through the partner, but it's not a traditional bank relationship — understand the structure, especially given the 2024 enforcement action.

providers.best_for: Contractors who want disciplined cash management — splitting every deposit into tax, materials, owner-pay, and profit accounts (Profit First) — with no-fee multi-account banking and crew debit cards
providers.weaknesses: Partner Thread Bank had a 2024 FDIC enforcement action and some users report account freezes; limited cash deposits; top savings APY requires a paid plan; multi-account system requires discipline to benefit

ratings.reddit_sentiment: top-ranked for Profit First / multi-account cash management; some concern about Thread Bank freezes

providers.affiliate_payout_note: No confirmed affiliate program — we currently earn nothing from Relay

faqs.q: What makes Relay different from other business banking?
faqs.a: Relay is built for cash management: you can open up to 20 checking accounts and 2 savings accounts under one login, each with its own account/routing number, and allocate every deposit across them — the Profit First method. That multi-account structure, plus up to 50 controlled debit cards, is its core differentiator versus single-account banking like Novo.

faqs.q: How much does Relay cost?
faqs.a: The Starter plan is free (0.91% savings APY, no minimum balance). Grow is $30/mo (1.55% APY) and Scale is $90/mo (2.68% APY). The multi-account structure is available on the free plan; the higher savings yields require a paid tier.

faqs.q: Is Relay safe given the Thread Bank issues?
faqs.a: Relay provides banking through Thread Bank (Member FDIC), which received an FDIC enforcement action in 2024, and some users report occasional account freezes with slow resolution. Customer funds remain FDIC-insured (up to $3M via deposit sweep), but it's prudent to keep a backup account and not run your only operating cash through any single fintech platform.

faqs.q: Can I deposit cash with Relay?
faqs.a: Cash deposits are limited, as with most fintech-banking platforms. If your trade takes meaningful cash, Relay (like Novo and Found) isn't ideal as your sole account — you'd need a traditional bank for cash handling. Relay shines for card/ACH/check-based businesses that want multi-account cash management.

faqs.q: Is Relay good for the Profit First method?
faqs.a: Yes — Relay is one of the most-recommended platforms for Profit First specifically, because its many sub-accounts let you automatically allocate each deposit into tax, owner-pay, materials, and profit buckets before you spend. If you follow (or want to adopt) Profit First, Relay is purpose-built for it.

providers.name: Bluevine
providers.tagline: Business checking with high APY plus a line of credit
providers.pricing_summary: No-fee Standard checking earning 1.3% APY; Plus ($30/mo) earns 1.75%; Premier ($95/mo) earns up to 3.0% APY (monthly fees waivable with balance/spend). Revolving line of credit up to $250,000 via partner bank. A fintech (banking via partner banks)
providers.apy: Up to 3.0% APY (1.3% Standard / 1.75% Plus / 3.0% Premier)
providers.long_description:
Bluevine pairs high-yield business checking with working-capital lending — the combination that sets it apart in this category. Founded in 2013 (headquartered in Jersey City), it's a fintech (banking via partner banks) whose Standard checking has no monthly fee and earns 1.3% APY, with Plus ($30/mo) at 1.75% and Premier ($95/mo) at up to 3.0% APY (the monthly fees are waivable with balance/spend requirements). On top of checking, Bluevine offers a revolving line of credit up to $250,000 (via Celtic Bank) with decisions often in minutes — genuinely useful for a contractor bridging payroll or materials between big jobs.

That banking-plus-credit pairing is the pitch for a growing trades business: park reserves and earn real interest, and have a working-capital line available without going to a separate lender. Its Trustpilot rating is solid (4.3/5 across 8,000+ reviews) from checking customers who praise the APY and app.

But Bluevine carries a serious, well-documented caveat: BBB complaints overwhelmingly describe an account-freeze and closure pattern, with users reporting accounts frozen after routine transactions and funds temporarily inaccessible. Funds are FDIC-insured via partner banks, but for a contractor that depends on cash flow, a surprise freeze is a real risk. The practical takeaway: Bluevine's APY and credit line are attractive, but don't run it as your only operating account — keep a backup, especially given the freeze pattern (and, like peers, limited cash deposits).
providers.how_it_works: You open a Bluevine business checking account online (a fintech account, FDIC-insured via partner banks). The Standard plan has no monthly fee and earns 1.3% APY; Plus ($30/mo) and Premier ($95/mo) earn higher APY (up to 3.0%) and waive their fees if you meet balance/spend thresholds. You get a debit card, bill pay, and no fees on incoming domestic wires/ACH or in-network ATMs. Separately, you can apply for a revolving line of credit up to $250,000 (through Celtic Bank), often with a decision in minutes, and draw on it for working capital. As with peers, plan around limited cash deposits and the documented account-freeze risk.

pros_detail.title: High APY on checking
pros_detail.explanation: Earn 1.3% APY on no-fee Standard checking, up to 3.0% on Premier — well above typical business checking. For a contractor holding tax/materials reserves, that idle cash actually earns meaningful interest.

pros_detail.title: Line of credit up to $250K
pros_detail.explanation: Bluevine's revolving line of credit (up to $250,000, via Celtic Bank, fast decisions) lets a contractor bridge payroll or materials between big jobs without a separate lender — banking and working capital in one place.

pros_detail.title: No-fee Standard plan
pros_detail.explanation: The Standard checking plan has no monthly fee plus no fees on incoming wires/ACH or in-network ATMs, so the entry point is genuinely low-cost while still earning APY.

pros_detail.title: Solid app and Trustpilot rating
pros_detail.explanation: A 4.3/5 Trustpilot rating across 8,000+ reviews reflects strong satisfaction among checking customers with the APY, app, and integrations.

pros_detail.title: Fee waivers at higher tiers
pros_detail.explanation: The Plus and Premier monthly fees are waivable if you meet balance/spend requirements, so a higher-volume shop can get top APY without effectively paying the fee.

cons_detail.title: Documented account-freeze pattern
cons_detail.explanation: BBB complaints overwhelmingly describe accounts frozen or closed after routine transactions, with funds temporarily inaccessible. For a contractor reliant on cash flow, a surprise freeze is a serious operational risk — don't make Bluevine your only account.

cons_detail.title: Polarized reviews
cons_detail.explanation: Reviews split sharply: checking users praise APY/app, while others report severe issues with frozen funds. The average rating hides a real bimodal experience — go in aware of the downside scenario.

cons_detail.title: Top APY requires paid tiers
cons_detail.explanation: 1.75% and 3.0% APY require Plus ($30/mo) or Premier ($95/mo). The fees are waivable with balances/spend, but the headline 3% isn't free for everyone.

cons_detail.title: Limited cash deposits
cons_detail.explanation: Like other fintech-banking platforms, cash deposits are limited — a constraint for cash-handling trades that want this as a primary operating account.

cons_detail.title: Fintech, not a chartered bank
cons_detail.explanation: Bluevine is a fintech with banking via partner banks and lending via Celtic Bank. Deposits are FDIC-insured through partners, but understand the structure — especially given the freeze complaints — before centralizing your cash.

providers.best_for: Growing trades businesses that want to earn real APY on reserves and have a working-capital line of credit available — while keeping a backup account given the freeze risk
providers.weaknesses: Well-documented account-freeze/closure complaints (don't use as your only account); top APY requires paid tiers; limited cash deposits; fintech (banking via partner banks)
providers.reputation_flag: BBB complaints overwhelmingly document an account-freeze and closure pattern — users report accounts frozen after routine transactions with funds temporarily inaccessible. Deposits are FDIC-insured via partner banks, but do not run Bluevine as your only operating account; keep a backup and a cash buffer.

ratings.reddit_sentiment: praised for APY/app by checking users; serious account-freeze complaints from others — polarized

providers.affiliate_payout_note: No confirmed affiliate program — we currently earn nothing from Bluevine

faqs.q: How much APY does Bluevine pay?
faqs.a: Bluevine's Standard checking (no monthly fee) earns 1.3% APY, Plus ($30/mo) earns 1.75%, and Premier ($95/mo) earns up to 3.0% APY. The monthly fees on Plus and Premier are waivable if you meet balance/spend requirements, so a higher-volume shop can reach top APY effectively fee-free.

faqs.q: Does Bluevine offer a line of credit?
faqs.a: Yes — Bluevine offers a revolving business line of credit up to $250,000 (through Celtic Bank), often with a decision in minutes. That banking-plus-lending combination is its main differentiator: you can hold checking and have working capital available in one place, useful for bridging payroll or materials between big jobs.

faqs.q: Is Bluevine safe to use?
faqs.a: Funds are FDIC-insured via partner banks, but Bluevine has a well-documented account-freeze and closure pattern in BBB complaints — accounts frozen after routine transactions with funds temporarily inaccessible. It's a real risk for cash-flow-dependent contractors, so don't run Bluevine as your only operating account; keep a backup bank and a cash buffer.

faqs.q: Can I deposit cash with Bluevine?
faqs.a: Cash deposits are limited, like most fintech-banking platforms. Cash-handling trades shouldn't rely on Bluevine as their sole account for that reason. It's best suited to businesses that operate via card, ACH, and checks and want the APY and credit line.

faqs.q: Bluevine vs Relay vs Novo?
faqs.a: Bluevine leads on APY (up to 3%) and a line of credit; Relay leads on multi-account Profit First cash management; Novo is the simplest free checking. All are fintechs with limited cash deposits. Choose Bluevine for yield + credit, Relay for disciplined cash allocation, Novo for free simplicity — and given freeze risks across the category, keep a backup account regardless.

providers.name: Mercury
providers.tagline: Modern free business banking with up to $5M FDIC — but no cash deposits
providers.pricing_summary: Free tier $0/mo, no minimums, no overdraft fees; free domestic + USD international wires; free QuickBooks/Xero/NetSuite. Paid: Plus $29.90/mo, Pro $299/mo (relationship manager, advanced invoicing).
providers.apy: No checking APY; treasury/savings yield available
providers.long_description:
Mercury is sleek, genuinely free online business banking with a standout safety feature: a sweep network that spreads deposits across partner banks for up to $5M in FDIC coverage — far above the standard $250K, which matters when a large project deposit lands. It bundles checking, savings, cards, wires, bill pay, and invoicing in one clean dashboard used by 300,000+ businesses, with free QuickBooks/Xero/NetSuite sync even on the free plan.

Two things make it a careful fit for trades. First and biggest: Mercury does not support cash deposits at all — a dealbreaker for any contractor who takes cash. Second, it's a fintech, not a bank (deposits sit at partner banks like Choice, Column, and Evolve), and like other fintechs it has documented sudden account-freeze/closure complaints. It also skews toward registered LLCs/corps and gates its best perks behind $299/mo or large balances. For a registered, paid-by-ACH/check/card contractor or agency that wants modern banking and big FDIC coverage, it's excellent; for a cash-handling shop, look elsewhere.
providers.how_it_works: Open an account fully online; deposits are held at FDIC-insured partner banks and swept across up to 20 of them for up to $5M coverage. Fund by incoming wire, ACH, or remote check deposit — but not cash. Manage everything (cards, wires, bill pay, invoicing, sub-accounts) from one dashboard, with free accounting integrations. There are no branches.

pros_detail.title: Truly free, no minimums
pros_detail.explanation: $0/mo with no minimum balances or overdraft fees — good for lean or seasonal trades cash flow.

pros_detail.title: Up to $5M FDIC via sweep
pros_detail.explanation: Deposits spread across partner banks for far more coverage than the standard $250K — reassuring for large project deposits.

pros_detail.title: Sub-accounts with rules
pros_detail.explanation: Create accounts with auto-transfer rules to set aside taxes or per-job funds, a basic Profit First-style workflow.

pros_detail.title: Built-in invoicing + bill pay
pros_detail.explanation: Bill clients and pay vendors from the same dashboard, with free domestic and USD international wires.

pros_detail.title: Free accounting sync
pros_detail.explanation: QuickBooks, Xero, and NetSuite integrations are free even on the no-cost plan, keeping books reconciled.

cons_detail.title: No cash deposits at all
cons_detail.explanation: Mercury explicitly does not support cash deposits — a hard dealbreaker for any contractor who collects cash.

cons_detail.title: No physical branches
cons_detail.explanation: It's online-only, so there's no teller or in-person help when something goes wrong.

cons_detail.title: A fintech, not a bank
cons_detail.explanation: Your money sits at partner banks (Choice, Column, Evolve); Mercury itself isn't FDIC-insured as a bank.

cons_detail.title: Account-freeze complaints
cons_detail.explanation: Like other fintechs, it has documented sudden account-freeze/closure complaints during compliance reviews.

cons_detail.title: Best perks gated / LLC-leaning
cons_detail.explanation: Skews to registered LLCs/corps, and the strongest features sit behind $299/mo or large balances.

providers.best_for: Registered, online-paid contractors and agencies (ACH/check/card, not cash) who want modern free banking and large FDIC coverage
providers.weaknesses: No cash deposits at all; no branches; a fintech relying on partner banks; documented account-freeze complaints; best perks gated behind $299/mo or large balances; skews to registered LLCs/corps
providers.reputation_flag: Documented pattern of sudden account freezes/closures during compliance reviews (a common fintech risk), and it does not accept cash deposits at all — a hard limitation for cash-handling trades.

ratings.reddit_sentiment: a favorite of startups and agencies for clean UX and $5M FDIC; the recurring warnings are sudden compliance freezes and no cash deposits

providers.affiliate_program: Yes — referral program ($250 each when a referred business deposits $10K in 90 days); advisor program up to $1,500/client
providers.affiliate_payout_note: affiliate_url stays TBD until the tracking link is confirmed

faqs.q: Can I deposit cash with Mercury?
faqs.a: No — Mercury does not support cash deposits at all. If your trades business collects any meaningful cash, Mercury is the wrong choice; look at a traditional bank (Chase, BofA, U.S. Bank) or a fintech that supports retail cash deposits (Lili, NorthOne). Mercury suits businesses paid by ACH, check, and card.

faqs.q: Is Mercury a real bank, and is my money safe?
faqs.a: Mercury is a fintech, not a bank — your deposits are held at FDIC-insured partner banks (Choice Financial, Column, Evolve) and swept across many of them for up to $5M in FDIC coverage. The money is insured against bank failure, but FDIC insurance does not protect against a compliance-driven account freeze, which is the main fintech risk to weigh.

faqs.q: Is Mercury free?
faqs.a: Yes — the core plan is $0/mo with no minimum balance or overdraft fees, including free domestic and USD international wires and free QuickBooks/Xero/NetSuite sync. Paid tiers (Plus $29.90/mo, Pro $299/mo) add relationship management and advanced invoicing, but most small trades stay on the free plan.

faqs.q: Is Mercury good for contractors?
faqs.a: It's good for registered contractors and agencies that get paid electronically and want modern, free banking with strong FDIC coverage and accounting sync. It's a poor fit for cash-collecting trades (no cash deposits) and for owners who want a branch to walk into. Match it to how you actually get paid.

providers.name: Lili
providers.tagline: Banking + automatic tax set-aside built for 1099 and solo trades
providers.pricing_summary: Core $0/mo, Pro ~$15/mo, Smart ~$35/mo, Premium ~$55/mo (intro discounts first 3 months). No minimum balance or opening deposit. Up to 4.00% APY savings; FDIC up to $3M via sweep.
providers.long_description:
Lili is business banking built for the solo operator and 1099 contractor, and its killer feature is automatic tax set-aside: it estimates and buckets money for taxes as you get paid, plus layers in invoicing, expense management, and bookkeeping/tax-prep tools that auto-sort expenses into tax categories. For a one-person plumbing, electrical, or handyman business that dreads quarterly taxes, that's a genuine pain-killer in a free app. It also supports cash deposits (via Green Dot retail) and pays up to 4.00% APY on savings.

The caveats are the usual neobank ones plus some Lili specifics: cash deposits carry a ~$4.95 retailer fee with low daily/monthly caps; the best tax/accounting tools are paywalled at $35–$55/mo; there are no branches and weekend support is email-only; and there's a documented account-closure/frozen-funds pattern, sometimes fast. It's a fintech (banking via Sunrise Banks). For a 1099 trade that wants banking and tax tools in one cheap app, Lili is one of the best fits; for a multi-employee business, it's too solo-focused.
providers.how_it_works: Open an account online with no minimum; banking is provided by Sunrise Banks. Money comes in by ACH, wire, mobile check, or cash at 90,000+ retail locations (via Green Dot). The app sets aside taxes automatically, sends invoices, categorizes expenses for tax time, and offers high-yield savings and a credit-builder. Higher tiers ($15–$55/mo) unlock fuller bookkeeping, unlimited invoicing, and bill pay.

pros_detail.title: Automatic tax set-aside
pros_detail.explanation: Lili buckets money for taxes as you earn — directly solving the #1 financial headache for 1099 trades and quarterly filers.

pros_detail.title: Supports cash deposits
pros_detail.explanation: Deposit cash at 90,000+ retail locations (Walmart, CVS, Walgreens, 7-Eleven) via Green Dot — unusual for a fintech.

pros_detail.title: Free Core tier, no minimum
pros_detail.explanation: Start at $0/mo with no minimum balance or opening deposit — accessible for a brand-new solo trade.

pros_detail.title: Built-in invoicing + reports
pros_detail.explanation: Send invoices and get P&L/cash-flow reporting (Smart tier+), bundling banking and light bookkeeping.

pros_detail.title: High-yield savings + integrations
pros_detail.explanation: Up to 4.00% APY savings plus QuickBooks, Xero, Stripe, Shopify, and Gusto connections.

cons_detail.title: Cash deposits carry a fee + low caps
cons_detail.explanation: Each retail cash deposit runs ~$4.95 with low daily/monthly limits — fine for occasional cash, not for cash-heavy shops.

cons_detail.title: Best tools are paywalled
cons_detail.explanation: Full bookkeeping, unlimited invoicing, and bill pay sit at $35–$55/mo, so the tax magic isn't all free.

cons_detail.title: No branches, email-only weekend support
cons_detail.explanation: It's app-only, and weekend support is email — slow when something urgent breaks.

cons_detail.title: Account-closure pattern
cons_detail.explanation: Documented sudden account closures/frozen funds (sometimes within hours), and it's not BBB-accredited.

cons_detail.title: Built for solo, not teams
cons_detail.explanation: Optimized for one-person businesses; multi-employee trades will outgrow its single-user focus.

providers.best_for: Freelance, 1099, and solo trades (one-person plumbing, electrical, handyman) who want banking plus automatic tax set-aside and invoicing in one cheap app
providers.weaknesses: Cash deposits carry ~$4.95 fee + low caps; best tax/accounting tools paywalled at $35–55/mo; no branches, email-only weekend support; account-closure/frozen-funds pattern; built for solo, not teams
providers.reputation_flag: Documented pattern of sudden account closures / frozen funds (sometimes within ~12 hours), and not BBB-accredited — the standard neobank compliance-freeze risk. Keep a backup account and sweep funds regularly.

ratings.reddit_sentiment: praised by 1099 workers for the automatic tax buckets and ease of use; the risk is sudden account closures common to neobanks

providers.affiliate_program: Refer-a-friend (reported ~$100 per qualified referral); no confirmed publisher affiliate program — verify directly
providers.affiliate_payout_note: affiliate_url stays TBD; referral terms unconfirmed on an owned page

faqs.q: How does Lili help with taxes?
faqs.a: Lili automatically estimates and sets aside money for your taxes as income arrives, and its higher tiers categorize expenses into tax buckets and generate reports — so a 1099 contractor isn't scrambling at quarterly-tax time. That automatic tax set-aside is Lili's signature feature and the main reason solo trades choose it.

faqs.q: Can I deposit cash into Lili?
faqs.a: Yes — unlike many fintechs, Lili supports cash deposits at 90,000+ retail locations (Walmart, CVS, Walgreens, 7-Eleven) through Green Dot. Each deposit carries about a $4.95 retailer fee and there are low daily/monthly caps, so it works for occasional cash but not for a cash-heavy operation.

faqs.q: Is Lili free?
faqs.a: The Core plan is $0/mo with no minimum balance. Paid tiers (Pro ~$15, Smart ~$35, Premium ~$55/mo) unlock the fuller bookkeeping, unlimited invoicing, bill pay, and advanced tax tools. The basic banking plus high-yield savings is free; the deeper accounting features are paid.

faqs.q: Is Lili a good business bank for a growing crew?
faqs.a: Less so — Lili is optimized for one-person and 1099 businesses, so a multi-employee trade with payroll and several users will likely outgrow it. For solo operators it's excellent; as you add staff, a small-business-checking option (Relay, NorthOne) or a traditional bank fits better.

providers.name: NorthOne
providers.tagline: Trades-targeted business checking with Envelopes for taxes and per-job budgeting
providers.pricing_summary: Standard $0/mo (no minimum); Plus ~$30/mo (cash back, higher APY, $15 wires, priority support). 3.00% APY checking. $50 minimum opening deposit. ACH to $50K/day; unlimited free incoming domestic wires; Allpoint fee-free ATMs.
providers.long_description:
NorthOne is one of the few business-banking apps that explicitly markets to trades — construction, HVAC, plumbing, electrical, trucking — and its standout is Envelopes: unlimited sub-accounts you can auto-fund by rule for taxes, payroll, or a specific job, making Profit First-style money management native. The Standard tier is free with no minimum, it pays 3.00% APY on checking, supports cash deposits (via Green Dot retail), and integrates with QuickBooks, Wave, Zoho Books, and Patriot — the accounting tools small trades actually use.

The caveats: cash deposits cost up to ~$4.95 each with only one fee refund per month on Standard (three on Plus); the best features sit behind the $30/mo Plus tier; it's a fintech (banking via The Bancorp Bank), not a chartered bank with branches; and its Trustpilot score is low (~2.4), dominated by the account-suspension/frozen-funds and slow-support complaints common to Bancorp-backed fintechs. For an app-first contractor who wants strong tax/job budgeting and takes some cash, NorthOne fits well — just keep a backup given the freeze risk.
providers.how_it_works: Open in-app with a $50 minimum; deposits are held at The Bancorp Bank (FDIC to $250K). Create unlimited free Envelopes that auto-fund by rule for taxes, payroll, or per-job set-asides, pay bills, deposit checks by photo, deposit cash at retail via Green Dot, and use fee-free Allpoint ATMs. Connect QuickBooks, Wave, Zoho Books, or Patriot to keep the books synced.

pros_detail.title: Unlimited Envelopes for budgeting
pros_detail.explanation: Auto-funded sub-accounts for taxes, payroll, and per-job set-asides make Profit First-style management native — a real fit for trades cash flow.

pros_detail.title: Explicitly built for trades
pros_detail.explanation: NorthOne markets to construction, HVAC, plumbing, and trucking, so the workflows and content speak to contractors.

pros_detail.title: Free Standard tier + 3.00% APY
pros_detail.explanation: No-minimum free tier that still pays 3.00% APY on checking and includes unlimited free incoming wires.

pros_detail.title: Supports cash deposits
pros_detail.explanation: Take cash to Green Dot retail locations — useful for trades that still collect some cash on jobs.

pros_detail.title: Real accounting integrations
pros_detail.explanation: Syncs with QuickBooks, Wave, Zoho Books, and Patriot, plus built-in bill pay — the tools small trades use.

cons_detail.title: Cash deposit fees + limited refunds
cons_detail.explanation: Up to ~$4.95 per cash deposit with only one fee refund/month on Standard (three on Plus) — not for cash-heavy shops.

cons_detail.title: Best features gated behind Plus
cons_detail.explanation: Cash back, higher APY, cheaper wires, and priority support require the $30/mo Plus tier.

cons_detail.title: A fintech, no branches
cons_detail.explanation: Banking is via The Bancorp Bank; there's no branch network or in-person help.

cons_detail.title: Low Trustpilot / freeze complaints
cons_detail.explanation: A ~2.4 Trustpilot score driven by account-suspension, frozen-funds, and slow-support complaints — the main risk to weigh.

cons_detail.title: Wire/limit constraints
cons_detail.explanation: Outgoing wires are capped and international wires aren't clearly advertised, which can constrain larger operations.

providers.best_for: App-first trades and contractors who want strong tax/job budgeting (Envelopes), accounting sync, and cash-deposit support — with a backup account given the freeze risk
providers.weaknesses: Cash deposit fees (~$4.95) with limited refunds; best features behind $30/mo Plus; a fintech with no branches; low Trustpilot (~2.4) from freeze/support complaints; capped wires
providers.reputation_flag: Low Trustpilot (~2.4) driven by a documented pattern of sudden account suspensions/closures, frozen funds, and slow support — the Bancorp/fintech freeze risk. Strong product, but keep a backup account and sweep funds.

ratings.reddit_sentiment: liked for trades-focused Envelopes/budgeting and cash support, but with consistent complaints about account suspensions and slow support

providers.affiliate_program: Referral — ~$75 per successful referral (both sides; referred deposits $50). Formal affiliate program unconfirmed
providers.affiliate_payout_note: affiliate_url stays TBD until a formal affiliate/referral link is confirmed

faqs.q: What are NorthOne Envelopes?
faqs.a: Envelopes are unlimited sub-accounts within your NorthOne checking that you can auto-fund by rule — for example, automatically routing a percentage of each deposit into a 'taxes,' 'payroll,' or per-job envelope. It makes Profit First-style money management native, which is why NorthOne appeals to trades managing lumpy project cash flow.

faqs.q: Does NorthOne accept cash deposits?
faqs.a: Yes — via Green Dot retail locations, at up to about $4.95 per deposit. Standard accounts get one fee refund per month and Plus accounts get three. It's workable for trades that take some cash, but the per-deposit fee and caps make it unsuitable for a heavily cash-based business.

faqs.q: Is NorthOne a bank?
faqs.a: No — NorthOne is a financial technology company; banking services are provided by The Bancorp Bank, N.A. (Member FDIC), with deposits insured to $250K. There are no physical branches. As with other fintechs, the main risk is a compliance-driven account freeze, which its Trustpilot reviews reflect — keep a backup account.

faqs.q: Is NorthOne good for contractors specifically?
faqs.a: Yes — it's one of the few business-banking apps that explicitly targets trades (construction, HVAC, plumbing, trucking), and the Envelopes feature plus QuickBooks/Wave/Zoho/Patriot integrations fit how contractors manage taxes, payroll, and job budgeting. The caveat is the freeze/support complaint pattern, so don't make it your only account.

providers.name: Chase Business Complete Banking
providers.tagline: Branches and cash everywhere, built-in card acceptance, full lending shelf
providers.pricing_summary: $15/mo, waived by ONE per cycle: $2,000 daily balance / $2,000 in QuickAccept card deposits / $2,000 Chase business-card spend / Private Client / military. Unlimited free electronic transactions. Signup bonus $400 ($2K new money) or $500 ($10K), 5 transactions in 90 days — expires 07/15/2026.
providers.monthly_fee: $15/mo (waivable by $2K balance, $2K card spend, or $2K QuickAccept deposits)
providers.long_description:
Chase Business Complete Banking is the strongest pick for cash-handling, card-accepting trades, because it pairs everyday checking with two things fintechs can't match: a nationwide branch and ATM network for in-person cash, and the full JPMorgan Chase credit-and-lending shelf — Ink business cards, lines of credit, SBA and equipment loans — for funding trucks and gear. It also has built-in card acceptance (QuickAccept) with same-day deposits, and Chase's app is rated #1 by J.D. Power for 2026.

The trade-offs are bank-style: $15/mo unless you keep a $2,000 balance, spend, or take $2,000 via QuickAccept; only $5,000 in free in-branch cash deposits per cycle (then $2.50/$1,000, though ATM cash deposits are unlimited and free); less native automation/bookkeeping than the fintechs; and documented complaints about abrupt account closures and extended check holds (plus a CFPB action). For a contractor who collects cash, accepts cards, and wants banking plus business credit under one roof with real branches, it's the benchmark — there's a live $400–$500 signup bonus through mid-July 2026.
providers.how_it_works: Open online or in a branch ($0 to open). The $15/mo fee auto-waives any cycle you hit a qualifying threshold (balance, card spend, or QuickAccept deposits). Deposit cash free in-branch up to $5,000/cycle (unlimited free at ATMs) and unlimited electronic transactions; take card payments via QuickAccept with same-day deposits; and tap Chase's lending products as you grow. Manage it through the top-rated Chase Business mobile app.

pros_detail.title: Branches and cash everywhere
pros_detail.explanation: ~4,700 branches accept in-person teller cash, and ~15,000+ ATMs take unlimited free cash deposits — the best cash option of the group.

pros_detail.title: Built-in card acceptance (QuickAccept)
pros_detail.explanation: Take cards with same-day deposits, and those deposits also help waive the monthly fee — two birds, one feature.

pros_detail.title: Full lending shelf
pros_detail.explanation: Ink cards, lines of credit, and SBA/equipment loans under one roof for financing trucks, tools, and growth.

pros_detail.title: #1-rated mobile app + Zelle
pros_detail.explanation: J.D. Power's #1 2026 business mobile app, plus Zelle for business and invoicing for getting paid.

pros_detail.title: Live signup bonus
pros_detail.explanation: A $400–$500 new-account bonus (through 07/15/2026) is real money for opening and using the account.

cons_detail.title: Monthly fee unless you qualify
cons_detail.explanation: $15/mo unless you keep a $2,000 balance, spend $2,000 on a Chase card, or take $2,000 via QuickAccept each cycle — a hurdle for seasonal trades.

cons_detail.title: Limited free in-branch cash
cons_detail.explanation: Only $5,000 in-branch cash free per cycle, then $2.50 per $1,000 (ATM cash deposits are unlimited/free).

cons_detail.title: Less automation than fintechs
cons_detail.explanation: No native tax envelopes or deep bookkeeping automation — it's a bank account, not a money-management app.

cons_detail.title: Abrupt-closure / hold complaints
cons_detail.explanation: Documented complaints about sudden account closures and extended check holds, plus a CFPB action over checking-screening reports.

cons_detail.title: No high-yield checking
cons_detail.explanation: Checking earns no interest, so idle balances don't work for you the way a fintech's APY would.

providers.best_for: Cash-handling and card-accepting trades who want branches, in-person cash deposits, and business credit/lending under one roof
providers.weaknesses: $15/mo unless waived; only $5,000 free in-branch cash/cycle; less automation/bookkeeping than fintechs; documented abrupt-closure/check-hold complaints + a CFPB action; no interest on checking
providers.reputation_flag: Documented complaints about abrupt account closures and extended check holds, plus a CFPB enforcement action over checking-screening reports. Standard big-bank risk — keep records and expect possible holds on large or unusual deposits.

ratings.reddit_sentiment: valued for branches, cash handling, and lending; gripes center on abrupt account closures and check holds typical of large banks

providers.affiliate_program: Chase Refer a Business ($100 per referred business, up to $1,000/yr) — a customer referral, not a publisher affiliate program
providers.affiliate_payout_note: affiliate_url stays TBD; this is a customer-referral, not a standard publisher affiliate

faqs.q: Is Chase good for a cash-based contractor?
faqs.a: Yes — it's the strongest pick here for cash. Chase has ~4,700 branches for in-person teller cash deposits and ~15,000+ ATMs that take unlimited free cash deposits. The catch is only $5,000 in-branch cash is free per cycle (then $2.50/$1,000), but ATM cash deposits are unlimited and free, which most cash-handling trades can work with.

faqs.q: How do I avoid the $15 monthly fee on Chase Business Complete?
faqs.a: Hit any one threshold per statement cycle: keep a $2,000 daily balance, take $2,000 in deposits via Chase QuickAccept card acceptance, spend $2,000 on a Chase Ink business card, link Chase Private Client, or qualify via military status. Meet one and the fee is waived that cycle.

faqs.q: Does Chase Business Complete let me take card payments?
faqs.a: Yes — QuickAccept is built in, letting you accept card payments (including via a mobile reader) with same-day deposits to your Chase account. Usefully, QuickAccept deposits of $2,000+/cycle also satisfy the monthly-fee waiver, so taking cards can keep the account free.

faqs.q: Is there a signup bonus?
faqs.a: Yes — as of mid-2026 Chase offers $400 for $2,000 in new money or $500 for $10,000, with 5 qualifying transactions in 90 days, expiring 07/15/2026. Bonus offers change, so confirm the current terms on Chase's site before opening.

providers.name: Bank of America Business Advantage Banking
providers.tagline: Traditional branch banking with Preferred Rewards that scale as you grow
providers.pricing_summary: Fundamentals $16/mo (waived by $5,000 avg balance / $500 debit purchases / Preferred Rewards; no fee until the 13th cycle for new accounts). Relationship $29.95/mo (waived at $15K balance or Preferred Rewards). $100 opening deposit. Signup bonus up to $750 online / up to $2,500 banker-introduced.
providers.apy: No interest on the checking tiers
providers.long_description:
Bank of America Business Advantage is the second traditional-bank pillar for trades: ~3,700 branches and a large ATM network for in-person cash and check deposits, plus the full BofA shelf of cards, lines of credit, SBA and term loans, and merchant services. Its differentiator is Preferred Rewards for Business — as your combined balances grow past $20K/$50K/$100K, it waives monthly fees on multiple accounts and adds 25–75% bonuses on business-card rewards, so the relationship pays off more the bigger you get.

The trade-offs are standard bank ones: a $16 (Fundamentals) or $29.95 (Relationship) monthly fee unless you hit a balance/spend/Preferred Rewards threshold; only $5,000 in free cash deposits per cycle on Fundamentals (then $0.30/$100); just 20 free transactions/cycle on Fundamentals; and the meaningful perks really need $20K+ parked. There's no native tax-envelope automation. But for a cash/check-handling trade that wants branch service and plans to consolidate cards, loans, and merchant services with one bank — especially carrying $5K–$20K+ balances — it's a strong, stable choice with a sizable signup bonus.
providers.how_it_works: Open online or in-branch under Fundamentals or Relationship; avoid the monthly fee by hitting a balance, debit-spend, or Preferred Rewards threshold each cycle. Deposit cash and checks at ~3,700 branches and ATMs. As combined business balances grow, Preferred Rewards tiers waive fees across multiple checking/savings accounts and boost card rewards. Manage via Business Advantage 360 with Cash Flow Monitor, Bill Pay, mobile check deposit, and Zelle for business.

pros_detail.title: Branches and cash/check deposits
pros_detail.explanation: ~3,700 branches plus a large ATM network for in-person cash and check deposits and same-day banker help.

pros_detail.title: Preferred Rewards scales with you
pros_detail.explanation: Growing balances waive fees on multiple accounts and add 25–75% to business-card rewards — the relationship pays off as you grow.

pros_detail.title: Full product shelf
pros_detail.explanation: Cards, lines of credit, SBA/term loans, merchant services, and payroll all available under one bank.

pros_detail.title: Solid digital tools + Zelle
pros_detail.explanation: Business Advantage 360, Cash Flow Monitor, Bill Pay, and Zelle for business, with QuickBooks sync.

pros_detail.title: Large signup bonus
pros_detail.explanation: Up to $750 online (or up to $2,500 banker-introduced) is among the bigger business-banking bonuses.

cons_detail.title: Monthly fee unless waived
cons_detail.explanation: $16 (Fundamentals) or $29.95 (Relationship) per month unless you hit a balance/spend/Preferred Rewards threshold — a hit for seasonal low-balance trades.

cons_detail.title: Limited free cash on Fundamentals
cons_detail.explanation: Only $5,000 cash free per cycle on Fundamentals, then $0.30 per $100 (more on Relationship).

cons_detail.title: Few free transactions on entry tier
cons_detail.explanation: Just 20 free transactions/cycle on Fundamentals before per-item fees apply.

cons_detail.title: Best perks need $20K+ parked
cons_detail.explanation: Preferred Rewards benefits really kick in at $20K+ combined balances — less useful for very small shops.

cons_detail.title: No native envelopes/automation
cons_detail.explanation: There's no built-in tax set-aside or sub-account automation like the fintechs offer.

providers.best_for: Cash/check-handling trades who want branch service and plan to consolidate cards, loans, and merchant services with one bank — especially carrying $5K–$20K+ balances
providers.weaknesses: $16/$29.95 monthly fee unless waived; only $5,000 free cash/cycle on Fundamentals; 20 free transactions/cycle on entry tier; best perks need $20K+ balances; no native envelopes/automation

ratings.reddit_sentiment: a stable traditional choice valued for branches and Preferred Rewards; common gripes are monthly fees if waivers are missed and a low free-cash cap

providers.affiliate_program: No business-checking refer-a-friend confirmed; acquisition incentive is the cash signup bonus
providers.affiliate_payout_note: affiliate_url stays TBD; primary incentive is the new-account bonus, not an affiliate link

faqs.q: How does Bank of America Preferred Rewards help a contractor?
faqs.a: As your combined business balances grow past $20K/$50K/$100K, Preferred Rewards for Business waives monthly fees on multiple accounts and boosts your business-card rewards by 25–75%. The bigger the banking relationship, the more it pays off — so it rewards trades that consolidate balances, cards, and loans at BofA rather than spreading them around.

faqs.q: What does Business Advantage cost and how do I avoid the fee?
faqs.a: Fundamentals is $16/mo, waived by a $5,000 average balance, $500 in debit-card purchases, or Preferred Rewards enrollment (and there's no fee until the 13th cycle for new accounts). Relationship is $29.95/mo, waived at $15K balance or via Preferred Rewards. Opening deposit is $100.

faqs.q: Can I deposit cash at Bank of America?
faqs.a: Yes — at ~3,700 branches and its ATM network. Fundamentals includes $5,000 in free cash deposits per statement cycle (then $0.30 per $100); the Relationship tier raises that allowance. For a cash-handling trade it's solid, though Chase's unlimited free ATM cash deposits edge it out at high cash volume.

faqs.q: Is there a signup bonus for Bank of America business checking?
faqs.a: Yes — up to $750 for opening online, or up to $2,500 through a banker introduction (tiered by the balance you bring), as of mid-2026 with deadlines through the year. Bonus terms change, so confirm the current offer and qualifying requirements on BofA's site before opening.

providers.name: U.S. Bank Business Essentials
providers.tagline: A genuinely free traditional-bank checking that takes cash and cards
providers.pricing_summary: $0 monthly fee, no minimum balance; $25 opening deposit. Unlimited digital transactions + 25 free teller/paper ($0.50 beyond). Free Visa debit, mobile check deposit, bill pay; fee-free at 13,000+ ATMs; built-in mobile card reader + same-day funds. Signup bonus $400 (promo, deposit $5,000 in 30 days).
providers.apy: No interest on the free tier
providers.long_description:
U.S. Bank Business Essentials is the rare traditional-bank account that's genuinely free — $0 monthly fee, no minimum balance — while still giving you branch access, cash handling, and built-in card-payment acceptance with same-day funds, all from a top-five national bank with a full lending shelf behind it. For a cash-collecting trade that wants no monthly fee plus the safety net of branches and SBA/equipment financing, it's a strong middle ground between a fintech and a fee-charging big-bank account. There's a live $400 signup bonus too.

The limits are around cash and transaction volume: only $2,500 (or 25 cash transactions) free per cycle, then $0.50 per unit, and 25 free teller/paper transactions before per-item fees — fine for low-to-moderate cash, tight for a heavily cash business. The Silver→Business Essentials rebrand also causes spec confusion (ignore legacy '125 transactions' figures), and the free tier earns no interest. But as a no-fee account that still takes cash and cards at a national bank, it's an easy recommendation for cost-conscious trades that want branches.
providers.how_it_works: Open online ($25 deposit) or in-branch. Get unlimited free digital transactions and 25 free teller/paper transactions per cycle; deposit cash and checks at ~2,000 branches or 13,000+ fee-free ATMs. A bundled free mobile card reader lets you take card payments on-site with same-day funds. Stay within the cash/teller allowances and the account remains free, with U.S. Bank's lending products available as you grow.

pros_detail.title: Genuinely free, no minimum
pros_detail.explanation: $0 monthly fee with no minimum balance — a real free tier from a major national bank, rare among traditional banks.

pros_detail.title: Accepts cash at branches + ATMs
pros_detail.explanation: Deposit cash at ~2,000 branches and 13,000+ fee-free ATMs — branch cash handling without a monthly fee.

pros_detail.title: Built-in card payments + same-day funds
pros_detail.explanation: A bundled free mobile card reader lets trades take cards on-site with same-day funding — banking and acceptance in one.

pros_detail.title: Full lending shelf
pros_detail.explanation: SBA loans, lines of credit, and equipment financing from a top-five bank are available as the business grows.

pros_detail.title: Live $400 signup bonus
pros_detail.explanation: A current $400 new-account bonus (deposit $5,000, hold 60 days, 6 transactions) adds real value for opening.

cons_detail.title: Low free-cash allowance
cons_detail.explanation: Only $2,500 or 25 cash transactions free per cycle, then $0.50 per unit — tight for a heavily cash-based trade.

cons_detail.title: Capped teller/paper transactions
cons_detail.explanation: Just 25 free teller/paper transactions per cycle before $0.50 each — watch it if you write/deposit many checks.

cons_detail.title: Rebrand spec confusion
cons_detail.explanation: The Silver→Business Essentials rename created conflicting specs online (ignore legacy '125 transactions' figures).

cons_detail.title: Bonus has conditions
cons_detail.explanation: The $400 bonus needs $5,000 parked ~60 days plus 6 qualifying transactions — not instant.

cons_detail.title: No interest on the free tier
cons_detail.explanation: The free account earns no interest, so idle balances don't earn the way a fintech APY would.

providers.best_for: Cost-conscious, cash-collecting trades that want a genuinely free account with branch access and built-in card payments from a national bank
providers.weaknesses: Only $2,500 / 25 cash transactions free per cycle; 25 free teller/paper transactions then $0.50 each; rebrand spec confusion; bonus needs $5K parked + 6 transactions; no interest on the free tier

ratings.reddit_sentiment: liked as a genuinely free traditional-bank account that still takes cash and includes card acceptance; the limit is the modest free-cash allowance

providers.affiliate_program: Public $400 new-account bonus; no consumer referral program confirmed
providers.affiliate_payout_note: affiliate_url stays TBD; primary incentive is the new-account bonus

faqs.q: Is U.S. Bank Business Essentials really free?
faqs.a: Yes — it has a $0 monthly fee and no minimum balance requirement (just a $25 opening deposit). You get unlimited free digital transactions and 25 free teller/paper transactions per cycle. It's one of the few genuinely free business checking accounts from a major national bank, which is its main appeal.

faqs.q: How much cash can I deposit for free?
faqs.a: Up to $2,500 or 25 cash transactions per statement cycle (whichever comes first), then $0.50 per unit (a unit is roughly each $100, with small deposits counting as one unit). That covers low-to-moderate cash volume; a heavily cash-based trade may prefer Chase's unlimited free ATM cash deposits.

faqs.q: Can I take card payments with U.S. Bank Business Essentials?
faqs.a: Yes — the account bundles a free mobile card reader so you can accept card payments on-site, with same-day funding to your account. That combination of a free checking account plus built-in card acceptance is unusual and handy for field trades.

faqs.q: What is the signup bonus?
faqs.a: As of mid-2026, U.S. Bank offers a $400 bonus on Business Essentials (open by the promo deadline, deposit $5,000 in new money within 30 days, keep it through day 60, and make 6 qualifying transactions). A higher Platinum-tier bonus exists for larger balances. Confirm current terms on U.S. Bank's site.
